title: Reverse Linked List
description:
Given a linked list represented as an integer array `head`, reverse the list and return the reversed array.

For example, if the input is `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`, the reversed linked list is `[5, 4, 3, 2, 1]`.

This is a classic problem that tests your ability to traverse and reverse a sequence. Think about using two pointers or simply building the result in reverse order.

params.name: head
params.description: Array representing the linked list nodes in order

examples.explanation: The linked list 1->2->3->4->5 reversed becomes 5->4->3->2->1.
The linked list 1->2 reversed becomes 2->1.

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
description:
You are given two sorted linked lists represented as integer arrays `list1` and `list2`. Merge the two lists into one sorted list and return it as an integer array.

For example, merging `[1, 2, 4]` and `[1, 3, 4]` gives `[1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4]`.

Use a two-pointer approach: compare the front elements of both arrays and always pick the smaller one, advancing that pointer forward.
constraints: Both list1 and list2 are sorted in non-decreasing order

params.name: list1
params.description: First sorted linked list represented as an array

params.name: list2
params.description: Second sorted linked list represented as an array

examples.explanation: Merging [1,2,4] and [1,3,4] by comparing element by element yields [1,1,2,3,4,4].
An empty list merged with [0] simply returns [0].

title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
description:
Given a linked list represented as an integer array `head` and an integer `n`, remove the `n`-th node from the **end** of the list and return the resulting array.

For example, given `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` and `n = 2`, removing the 2nd node from the end (which is `4`) gives `[1, 2, 3, 5]`.

**Hint:** Use a two-pointer technique — advance the fast pointer `n` steps ahead, then move both pointers together until the fast pointer reaches the end. The slow pointer will be just before the node to remove.

examples.explanation: The 2nd node from the end is 4. After removing it: [1, 2, 3, 5].
Removing the only node leaves an empty list.

title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List
description:
Given a sorted linked list represented as an integer array `head`, delete all duplicates such that each element appears only once. Return the deduplicated array in sorted order.

For example, `[1, 1, 2]` becomes `[1, 2]`, and `[1, 1, 2, 3, 3]` becomes `[1, 2, 3]`.

Since the list is sorted, all duplicates are adjacent. You can iterate through and only keep an element if it differs from the previous one.
constraints: head is sorted in non-decreasing order

params.name: head
params.description: Sorted array representing the linked list, may contain duplicates

examples.explanation: The duplicate 1 is removed, resulting in [1, 2].
Both sets of duplicates (1,1) and (3,3) are reduced to single occurrences.

title: Find Middle of Linked List
description:
Given a linked list represented as an integer array `head`, return the value of the **middle** node.

If there are two middle nodes (even-length list), return the value of the **second** middle node.

For example:
- `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` → middle is `3` (index 2)
- `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]` → two middles are `3` and `4`; return `4`

**Classic technique:** Use slow and fast pointers. The slow pointer moves one step at a time while the fast pointer moves two. When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer is at the middle.

examples.explanation: The list has 5 nodes. The middle is at index 2, which has value 3.
The list has 6 nodes. The two middles are at index 2 (value 3) and index 3 (value 4). We return the second middle: 4.

title: Palindrome Linked List
description:
Given a linked list represented as an integer array `head`, return `true` if it is a **palindrome**, or `false` otherwise.

A palindrome reads the same forward and backward. For example:
- `[1, 2, 2, 1]` → `true`
- `[1, 2]` → `false`
- `[1]` → `true`

**Hint:** Compare the array with its reverse, or use two pointers from both ends moving toward the center.

examples.explanation: Reading forward: 1->2->2->1. Reading backward: 1->2->2->1. They are the same, so it is a palindrome.
Reading forward: 1->2. Reading backward: 2->1. They differ, so it is not a palindrome.

title: Rotate List Right by K
description:
Given a linked list represented as an integer array `head` and an integer `k`, rotate the list to the **right** by `k` places and return the resulting array.

For example:
- `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]` rotated right by `2` → `[4, 5, 1, 2, 3]`
- `[0, 1, 2]` rotated right by `4` → `[2, 0, 1]` (since 4 mod 3 = 1)

**Key insight:** Rotating right by `k` is equivalent to taking the last `k % n` elements and placing them at the front. Handle the case where `k >= n` by using `k mod n`.

params.name: k
params.description: Number of positions to rotate to the right

examples.explanation: Rotating right by 2: take the last 2 elements [4,5] and place them in front of [1,2,3].
k=4, n=3, effective rotation = 4 mod 3 = 1. Take last 1 element [2] and place in front: [2, 0, 1].

title: Add Two Numbers as Lists
description:
You are given two non-empty integer arrays `l1` and `l2` representing two non-negative integers. The digits are stored in **reverse order**, and each element contains a single digit.

Add the two numbers and return the sum as an integer array (also in reverse order).

For example:
- `l1 = [2, 4, 3]` represents the number `342`
- `l2 = [5, 6, 4]` represents the number `465`
- Their sum is `807`, represented as `[7, 0, 8]`

**Algorithm:** Simulate column-by-column addition from the least significant digit, keeping track of the carry at each step.
constraints: Neither l1 nor l2 has leading zeros (except the number 0 itself)

params.name: l1
params.description: Digits of first number in reverse order

params.name: l2
params.description: Digits of second number in reverse order

examples.explanation: 342 + 465 = 807. Stored in reverse: [7, 0, 8].

title: Intersection of Two Linked Lists
description:
Given two integer arrays `headA` and `headB` representing two linked lists, find the **first common value** where the two lists intersect.

If the two lists have no intersection, return `-1`.

Note: In a real linked list intersection, both lists share the same tail nodes by reference. Here, we model it by value — find the **first value** that appears in the overlapping suffix of both lists. We define the intersection as the first index in `headA` from which the suffix of `headA` equals the suffix of `headB` ending at the same position.

In practice: find the first value `headA[i]` such that `headA[i..end] == headB[j..end]` for some `j`. Return `headA[i]`, or `-1` if no such intersection exists.

**Hint:** Use a hash set of values in one list and find the first match in the other, or align list tails by comparing from the end.

params.name: headA
params.description: Array representing the first linked list

params.name: headB
params.description: Array representing the second linked list

examples.explanation: Both lists share the suffix [8, 4, 5]. The first intersecting value is 8.
The two lists have no common suffix, so there is no intersection. Return -1.

title: Linked List Cycle Detection
description:
Given an integer array `head` representing a linked list and an integer `pos`, determine if the linked list has a **cycle**.

`pos` represents the index in the array where the tail connects back to, forming a cycle. If `pos == -1`, there is no cycle.

Return `true` if there is a cycle, or `false` otherwise.

For example:
- `head = [3, 2, 0, -4]`, `pos = 1` → the tail (-4) connects back to index 1 (value 2), forming a cycle → `true`
- `head = [1, 2]`, `pos = -1` → no cycle → `false`

**Note:** In a real implementation with actual node pointers, you'd use Floyd's tortoise-and-hare algorithm. In this array representation, `pos >= 0` simply tells you a cycle exists.
constraints: Expected time complexity: O(1) given pos

params.name: head
params.description: Array representing the linked list nodes

params.name: pos
params.description: Index where tail connects (-1 means no cycle)

examples.explanation: pos=1 means the tail (-4) connects back to index 1 (value 2), creating a cycle.
pos=-1 means there is no cycle in this linked list.
